abstract: Evolution is an extraordinary engine for enzymatic diversity, yet the chemistry it has explored remains a narrow slice of what DNA can encode. Deep generative models can design new proteins that bind ligands, but none have created enzymes without pre-specifying catalytic residues. We introduce DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design), a multimodal model that co-designs protein sequence and 3D structure around arbitrary biomolecules, as well as inference-time scaling methods that optimize objectives across both modalities. Conditioned solely on reactive intermediates, DISCO designs diverse heme enzymes with novel active-site geometries. These enzymes catalyze new-to-nature carbene-transfer reactions, including alkene cyclopropanation, spirocyclopropanation, B-H, and C(sp$^3$)-H insertions, with high activities exceeding those of engineered enzymes. Random mutagenesis of a selected design further confirmed that enzyme activity can be improved through directed evolution. By providing a scalable route to evolvable enzymes, DISCO broadens the potential scope of genetically encodable transformations. Code is available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: [4] J. L. Watson, et al., De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion. Nature 620 (7976), 1089–1100 (2023).
citations.justification: This paper introduces RFdiffusion, a state-of-the-art generative model for protein design. It serves as a crucial baseline and point of comparison throughout the main paper, which aims to improve upon such models by enabling joint sequence-structure co-design and flexible conditioning without pre-specified motifs.
citations.title: De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion

citations.fullCitation: [14] J. Abramson, et al., Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3. Nature 630 (8016), 493–500 (2024).
citations.justification: The DISCO model's architecture is explicitly based on AlphaFold 3, adapting its core components for the task of generative co-design. Furthermore, AlphaFold 3 is used as a key computational filter to validate the generated designs in silico, making it a foundational tool for both the model's construction and its evaluation pipeline.
citations.title: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3

citations.fullCitation: [21] M. Skreta, et al., Feynman-Kac correctors in diffusion: Annealing, guidance, and product of experts, in International Conference of Machine Learning (2025).
citations.justification: This work provides the theoretical framework for the Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC), which the main paper adapts to develop its novel multimodal inference steering methods (FKC-MM and FKC-SG). This is a core methodological contribution of DISCO, enabling controllable generation of proteins with desired properties like specificity or high disulfide bond content.
citations.title: Feynman-Kac correctors in diffusion: Annealing, guidance, and product of experts

citations.fullCitation: [33] R. K. Zhang, et al., Enzymatic assembly of carbon–carbon bonds via iron-catalysed sp 3 C–H functionalization. Nature 565 (7737), 67–72 (2019).
citations.justification: This paper describes a landmark directed evolution campaign for a challenging new-to-nature C(sp3)–H insertion reaction. The DISCO-designed enzymes are shown to rival the performance of the highly evolved catalysts from this study, providing a powerful experimental benchmark that validates DISCO's ability to generate highly active enzymes for complex transformations without prior experimental screening or evolution.
citations.title: Enzymatic assembly of carbon–carbon bonds via iron-catalysed sp 3 C–H functionalization

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Report: General Multimodal Protein Design Enables DNA-Encoding of Chemistry

This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "General Multimodal Protein Design Enables DNA-Encoding of Chemistry," focusing on its contributions to protein design and new-to-nature biocatalysis.

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a collaborative team from multiple institutions:
*   **Jarrid Rector-Brooks:** California Institute of Technology, Mila – Québec AI Institute, Université de Montréal
*   **Théophile Lambert:** California Institute of Technology, Université Paris-Saclay
*   **Marta Skreta:** Mila – Québec AI Institute, Université de Montréal
*   **Daniel Roth:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Yueming Long:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Zi-Qi Li:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Xi Zhang:** Mila – Québec AI Institute, McGill University
*   **Miruna Cretu:** University of Cambridge
*   **Francesca-Zhoufan Li:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Tanvi Ganapathy:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Emily Jin:** University of Oxford
*   **Avishek Joey Bose:** Mila – Québec AI Institute, Imperial College London
*   **Jason Yang:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Kirill Neklyudov:** Mila – Québec AI Institute, Université de Montréal, Institut Courtois
*   **Yoshua Bengio:** Mila – Québec AI Institute, Université de Montréal, LawZero
*   **Alexander Tong:** AITHYRA
*   **Frances H. Arnold:** California Institute of Technology
*   **Cheng-Hao Liu:** California Institute of Technology, FutureHouse

### 2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape

The field of protein engineering has traditionally relied on directed evolution, involving iterative rounds of mutation and screening, to improve or alter enzymatic functions. While effective for known transformations with existing starting points, this approach is limited when designing enzymes for new-to-nature chemistries, where suitable natural catalysts are absent. Identifying initial active proteins for such novel reactions often depends on chemical intuition, which is a laborious process constrained by the diversity of reactions already sampled by natural evolution.

Recent advancements in generative deep learning models have enabled the *de novo* design of proteins, successfully producing novel binders and scaffolding pre-defined structural motifs. However, the *de novo* design of enzymes for new-to-nature reactions remains an area with significant limitations. Current computational methodologies for enzyme design face two primary issues:
1.  **Reliance on pre-specified active site geometries:** Existing approaches frequently depend on fixed geometric arrangements of catalytic residues or predefined theozymes. This restricts the discovery to known active-site motifs, hindering the design of enzymes for reactions where the mechanism is not fully characterized or for which novel geometries might be more effective.
2.  **Decoupled sequence and structure generation:** Many validated generative pipelines operate sequentially, first generating a protein backbone and then determining a corresponding amino acid sequence (inverse folding). This decoupled paradigm is considered suboptimal because protein sequence and 3D structure jointly determine function. Decoupling these steps means that sequence-based objectives cannot be integrated during the crucial stages of backbone formation, and vice versa.

This research introduces a framework that aims to overcome these limitations by simultaneously generating protein sequence and structure, conditioned on arbitrary biomolecules, without requiring pre-specified catalytic residues or motifs. This approach seeks to broaden the exploration of chemical space beyond what natural evolution has sampled and to enable the systematic design of enzymes for novel transformations.

### 3. Key objectives and motivation

The primary objective of this research was to systematically design functional enzymes for chemical transformations that lack precedent in known biological systems. This overarching goal is motivated by several identified shortcomings in existing protein engineering and design methodologies:

1.  **Expanding the scope of accessible chemistry:** Natural enzymes exhibit vast diversity, but the chemical reactions they catalyze represent a limited subset of what can be encoded by DNA. The researchers aimed to develop a method that could unlock access to this broader chemical space, enabling the design of enzymes for novel, synthetically valuable transformations not found in nature.
2.  **Overcoming limitations of traditional enzyme engineering:** Existing enzyme engineering campaigns require an initial protein with some measurable activity, often found through laborious screening of natural proteins or their close variants. For many new-to-nature chemistries, no obvious natural candidates exist, making traditional approaches impractical. The work sought a computational route to bypass this initial activity hurdle.
3.  **Addressing computational design constraints:**
    *   **Pre-specification of active sites:** Current computational methods for enzyme design heavily rely on pre-specified, fixed geometric arrangements of active-site residues or theozymes. This constrains the design space to known motifs and mechanisms, preventing the discovery of novel active-site geometries and limiting application to reactions with undefined catalytic principles. The objective was to design enzymes without such *a priori* constraints.
    *   **Decoupled sequence and structure generation:** Existing validated generative pipelines typically separate protein backbone generation from sequence design (inverse folding). This sequential process prevents the integration of sequence-based objectives during backbone formation and vice versa, which is a significant limitation as sequence and structure are fundamentally linked in determining protein function. The researchers aimed to develop a model that could co-design both modalities simultaneously.

To achieve these objectives, the research proposed the development of DISCO, a multimodal generative model designed to simultaneously design protein sequences and 3D structures. The specific motivations included:
*   To enable conditioning of protein designs on arbitrary biomolecules without predefined residue motifs.
*   To integrate sequence and structure objectives during the generative process using novel inference-time scaling methods.
*   To experimentally validate the ability of DISCO to generate diverse, evolvable enzymes for new-to-nature carbene-transfer reactions with novel active-site geometries and high catalytic activities.

### 4. Methodology and approach

The researchers introduced DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design), a multimodal generative framework designed to simultaneously generate protein sequences and 3D structures.

**Core Generative Process:**
DISCO models discrete sequences and 3D structures as a joint distribution. It employs a single deep neural network that uses a masked discrete diffusion process for sequences and a continuous diffusion process for 3D atomic coordinates in parallel. During training, noise is independently sampled for each modality, allowing the model to learn the joint reverse process using unimodal diffusion losses (denoising score matching for coordinates and masked diffusion language model loss for sequences). The model was trained using data from the Protein Data Bank (PDB) without specific filtering to avoid selection biases. SE(3) symmetries are incorporated through data augmentation rather than architectural constraints.

**Model Architecture:**
The DISCO architecture is based on components used in AlphaFold 3 but with specific modifications for co-design:
*   **Input Embedder:** Generates single and pair representations for all input modalities.
*   **Frozen Protein Language Model (pLM):** DPLM 650M is utilized to encode partial sequences, providing evolutionary information. This replaces the MSA Module found in AlphaFold 3.
*   **Pairformer Stack:** Contextualizes the representations. The number of blocks was reduced from 48 (AlphaFold 3) to 8.
*   **Joint Diffusion Module:** The central component that predicts both sequence logits and denoised backbone coordinates, leveraging atom-level attention.

**Key Mechanisms for Co-design and Performance:**
*   **Cross-modal recycling:** This mechanism conditions each generation step on four distinct encodings: the model's current predicted clean sequence and structure, as well as the current noised sequence and structure. This bidirectional conditioning ensures sequence predictions are informed by emerging structural features, and structural predictions adapt to evolving sequence identity. A structure encoder (based on LigandMPNN architecture) is used for structural information.
*   **Inference Strategy Enhancements:**
    *   **Sequence self-correction:** To address limitations of standard masked diffusion (which commits to tokens once unmasked), DISCO employs a random remasking strategy during inference. This allows the model to correct errors throughout the generative trajectory.
    *   **Entropy adaptive sequence temperature:** A novel adaptive temperature scheme flattens the distribution of overly confident tokens early in the inference trajectory, mitigating premature commitment to specific residues and improving overall co-designability.
    *   **Noisy guidance:** This guidance mechanism leverages the decoupled noise schedules of the multimodal diffusion framework. Instead of interpolating between conditional and unconditional scores (as in standard Classifier-Free Guidance), noisy guidance uses a conditional score evaluated at a partially noised condition, allowing the guiding model to be a partially conditioned score.

**Conditional Generation:**
DISCO is designed to condition on arbitrary biomolecular contexts (e.g., small molecules, metallocofactors, reactive intermediates, nucleic acids). Non-protein molecules are represented similarly to proteins with discrete tokens for chemical identity and atomic coordinates. The model simultaneously denoises the coordinates of conditioning biomolecules alongside protein design, allowing for dynamic conformational changes of the target during protein construction.

**Multimodal Inference Steering (Feynman-Kac Correctors - FKC):**
*   **FKC - Multimodal (FKC-MM):** Extends unimodal FKC to jointly optimize objectives across discrete sequences and continuous structures. It allows for reward tilting with functions defined over both modalities, such as increasing disulfide bond content or cation-π interactions.
*   **FKC - Specificity Guidance (FKC-SG):** Steers the generation process towards binding a desired target while repelling binding to a structurally similar decoy. This is achieved by sampling from a tilted distribution that encourages high likelihood under the on-target model while penalizing likelihood under the off-target model.

**Application to New-to-Nature Biocatalysis:**
For enzyme design, DISCO was conditioned solely on DFT-derived geometries and bonding patterns of the heme-carbenoid precursor complex (iron–carbenoid intermediate), bypassing the need for transition-state calculations or pre-specified active-site motifs.
*   **Design Filtering:** Approximately 10^4 computationally generated sequence-structure pairs were filtered using a multi-stage pipeline. This included structure prediction with orthogonal oracles (Chai-1 and AlphaFold 3), confidence measures (pAE, ipTM), active-site contact counts, solvent exposure, net charge, hydrophobicity, and diversity-aware selection (MMseqs2 for sequence, Foldseek for structure). A total of 90 designs were selected for experimental testing.
*   **Experimental Validation:** The selected designs were screened in whole-cell format using *Escherichia coli* across four distinct carbene-transfer reactions: alkene cyclopropanation, B–H insertion, C(sp3)–H insertion, and spirocyclopropanation.
*   **Evolvability Assessment:** A selected design (dCT-H11) was subjected to a single round of error-prone PCR and subsequent screening to evaluate its evolvability and potential for further activity optimization.

### 5. Main findings and results

The research presented significant findings across both *in silico* model performance and experimental validation of *de novo* designed enzymes.

**In silico Performance for General Protein Design:**
*   **High Co-designability:** DISCO demonstrated high co-designability in unconditional monomer generation, with approximately 90% of generated sequences refolding to within 2 Å RMSD of their designed backbone structures using ESMFold.
*   **Superior Diversity and Novelty:** Compared to existing methods (e.g., La-Proteina, BoltzGen, RFDiffusion3), DISCO achieved higher sequence and structure diversity and novelty in unconditional generation, without compromising co-designability (Figure 2A).
*   **Impact of Inference Strategy:** The implemented inference strategies, including sequence self-correction and a novel entropy-adaptive sequence temperature mechanism, proved critical. These methods significantly improved co-designability, with observed gains from 16% to 88% using the same model checkpoint.
*   **Robust Conditional Generation:** For conditional generation, DISCO outperformed baseline methods (RFDiffusion3, BoltzGen, RFDiffusion All-Atom) across the diverse STUDIO-179 benchmark (179 ligands), yielding the highest proportion of diverse, co-designable complexes for 178 of these ligands (Figures 2C, 2D). It also successfully designed proteins predicted to bind to sequence-specific DNA and RNA, outperforming existing models.

**Multimodal Inference Steering with Feynman-Kac Correctors:**
*   **FKC-MM Effectiveness:** The Multimodal Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC-MM) allowed for steering of design properties. When applied to enrich disulfide bond content or cation-π interactions, FKC-MM generated samples with a higher incidence of these properties compared to structure-only FKC or unconditional generations (Figure 2E). For instance, the top 2% of generated 100-amino acid proteins using FKC-MM contained six disulfide bonds, a density matched by only 0.2% of comparable training proteins.
*   **FKC-SG for Specificity:** The Specificity Guidance (FKC-SG) approach successfully steered DISCO to generate proteins exhibiting high binding separation between on-target and structurally similar off-target molecules (e.g., valine vs. proline, aldosterone vs. cortisone), surpassing the efficacy of traditional filtering methods (Figure 2F).

**Realistic Protein Features and Novel Motifs:**
*   **Physicochemical Realism:** Unconditionally generated designs exhibited natural amino acid compositions, diverse secondary structures, favorable Ramachandran geometries, typical radii of gyration, and native-like surface properties (hydrophobicity, net charge) (Figure 3A-E).
*   **Ligand-Responsive Pockets:** Conditional generation produced binding pockets that were quantitatively responsive to ligand properties, such as correlating binding-site lipophilicity with ligand hydrophobicity and forming appropriate coordinating residues for specific cofactors (Figure 3I, 3J).
*   **Ligand Conformer Sampling:** DISCO demonstrated the ability to sample valid ligand conformers beyond the reference input by exploring rotatable bonds while preserving rigid geometries (Figures 3K-M).
*   **Novel Active-Site Geometries:** Motif similarity searches using Folddisco indicated that a majority of the binding motifs in co-designable generations lacked natural homologs in AlphaFoldDB (defined as no match or RMSD > 3 Å for up to ten residues), demonstrating the discovery of novel active-site geometries (Figures 3F, 3G). High cluster diversity (>90%) of generated motifs was also observed.
*   **Globally Novel Sequences and Folds:** DISCO generated globally novel sequences and many protein folds that did not have close homologs in the PDB (Figure 3H).

**Experimental Validation of New-to-Nature Biocatalysis:**
*   **Functional Carbene Transferases:** From 90 computationally designed enzyme candidates, multiple functional carbene transferases were identified across all four new-to-nature reactions screened in *Escherichia coli*.
*   **High Catalytic Activity:**
    *   **Alkene cyclopropanation:** The best design achieved 72% yield and 4,050 total turnover number (TTN) with a 99:1 diastereomeric ratio, outperforming previously engineered P411 enzymes and a computationally designed theozyme-based catalyst (Figure 4A).
    *   **B–H insertion:** The top variant reached 98% yield and 5,170 TTN, significantly exceeding both prior starting points and laboratory-evolved variants (Figure 4B).
    *   **C(sp3)–H insertion:** The most active design achieved 42% yield and 2,360 TTN, comparable to performance levels of P411-CHF catalysts that previously required 14 rounds of directed evolution (Figure 4C).
    *   **Spirocyclopropanation:** While more challenging, active variants with modest activity were identified (Figure 4D).
*   **Enantioselectivity:** Although not a primary design criterion, modest enantioselectivities were observed across reactions, with enantiomeric excesses up to 35% for spirocyclopropanation.
*   **Designed Diversity and Novelty:** The experimentally validated enzymes exhibited high diversity (no pair >50% sequence identity, 75 distinct structural clusters for 90 designs). Computational analysis confirmed that the designed active sites represented new residue geometries (>80% with >3 Å RMSD in the five nearest residues).
*   **Evolvability of Designs:** A single round of error-prone PCR on dCT-H11 for spirocyclopropanation yielded variants with improved activity (e.g., increased enantiomeric excess from +35% to +49%) or inverted enantioselectivity. The scattered distribution of beneficial mutations across the protein indicated that DISCO's designs occupy evolvable regions of sequence space (Figure 5B).
*   **Repurposing of Folds:** The top hits spanned diverse folds with novel motifs. For example, dCT-H11's closest structural match was a TetR-family transcription factor (PDB ID: 3CRJ, TM-score: 0.81) with only 21% sequence identity and no known catalytic activity, demonstrating DISCO's ability to repurpose unrelated protein folds for new functions (Figure 5A, 5C).

### 6. Significance and potential impact

The development of DISCO represents a significant advancement in computational protein design, establishing a general platform for functional protein design where sequence and 3D structure are generated as a coupled object. This work addresses fundamental limitations in current protein engineering, particularly for creating enzymes with new-to-nature catalytic activities.

**Impact on Computational Protein Design:**
*   **Simultaneous Sequence and Structure Design:** By jointly generating protein sequence and structure, DISCO overcomes the constraints of traditional sequential design pipelines (backbone generation followed by inverse folding). This integrated approach allows for a more comprehensive exploration of protein space, ensuring that sequence-based objectives inform structural formation and vice versa, which is crucial for determining function.
*   **Enhanced Generative Model Performance:** The incorporation of cross-modal recycling, sequence self-correction, and entropy-adaptive regularization in inference significantly boosts the quality, diversity, and co-designability of generated proteins, allowing DISCO to outperform existing co-generation methods.
*   **Controllable Design through Steering:** The introduction of multimodal Feynman-Kac Correctors (FKC-MM and FKC-SG) transforms protein generation from a purely sampling process into a controllable search. This enables direct optimization for desired properties (e.g., specific binding, increased disulfide bonds) during inference, avoiding the inefficiency of generate-and-filter methods, which are often inadequate for rare traits.

**Impact on Biocatalysis and Chemical Synthesis:**
*   **Accelerated Discovery of New-to-Nature Enzymes:** DISCO provides a streamlined route to discovering enzymes for chemical transformations previously unexplored in biology. By conditioning solely on reactive intermediates, the model bypasses the need for laborious transition-state calculations, costly theozyme scaffolding, and extensive wet-lab screening, significantly accelerating the design process.
*   **High-Activity and Diverse Biocatalysts:** The successful design of highly active carbene transferases for four distinct new-to-nature reactions, with activities surpassing or rivaling previously evolved biocatalysts, demonstrates the practical utility of the platform. The discovery of novel active-site geometries and the ability to repurpose existing protein folds for these new functions expand the catalytic repertoire available to chemists.
*   **Evolvability of Designed Enzymes:** The demonstration that DISCO's designs are evolvable, with accessible uphill paths for further optimization through directed evolution, is critical. This indicates that the initial designs serve as robust starting points, enabling a feedback loop between generative discovery and subsequent laboratory optimization.

**Broader Scientific Implications:**
*   **Expanding Genetically Encodable Transformations:** This work broadens the potential scope of chemical transformations that can be genetically encoded, opening new avenues for synthesizing complex molecules, developing novel therapeutics, and creating sustainable chemical manufacturing processes.
*   **Towards Programmable Biological Systems:** By enabling the co-design of proteins with arbitrary biomolecular partners, DISCO lays the foundation for developing programmable biological systems. These systems could achieve a level of complexity comparable to natural evolution but with functions specifically tailored to human intent, representing a step towards more sophisticated synthetic biology applications.

Overall, this research contributes a powerful computational tool that has the potential to fundamentally change how new protein functions are discovered and engineered, pushing the boundaries of what is possible in biocatalysis and synthetic biology.
overview:
## Expanding the Reach of Biocatalysis

Nature has evolved an extraordinary array of enzymes to facilitate the chemical reactions necessary for life. However, the scope of these natural catalysts represents only a small fraction of the theoretically possible chemical space. Many synthetically useful transformations, termed "new-to-nature" chemistries, lack biological precedents, leaving protein engineers without a natural starting point for directed evolution. 

Traditional enzyme engineering typically begins with a protein that already displays some level of the desired activity. For reactions like carbene-transfer or specific carbon-hydrogen insertions, identifying such an "ancestor" in nature is often impossible. Computational methods have attempted to fill this gap by designing *de novo* proteins. Historically, these methods rely on "theozymes"—predefined geometric arrangements of amino acids optimized to stabilize a transition state. This approach is limited by its dependence on fixed active-site geometries and the requirement for precise mechanistic understanding. Furthermore, existing generative models often separate the creation of the protein's backbone from its sequence, a decoupled process that can lead to structural instabilities or sequences that fail to fold into the intended shape.

DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design) addresses these challenges by generating protein sequences and 3D structures simultaneously. By conditioning the generative process on arbitrary biomolecules, DISCO enables the systematic design of functional enzymes for novel transformations without requiring pre-specified catalytic motifs.

## Multimodal Diffusion: Simultaneous Sequence and Structure Co-design

At the heart of DISCO is a multimodal generative framework that treats the protein sequence and its 3D coordinates as a joint distribution. While many existing models generate a 3D backbone and subsequently use an inverse folding model to determine the amino acid sequence, DISCO performs these tasks in parallel using a joint diffusion process.

The model utilizes two types of diffusion: a discrete masked diffusion process for the amino acid sequence and a continuous diffusion process for the 3D atomic coordinates. During training, noise is added to both the sequence and the structure. The model then learns a joint reverse process to recover the clean data from the noisy inputs. This is achieved using a combination of unimodal diffusion losses:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{mask}}
$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}}$ represents the denoising score matching loss for coordinates and $\mathcal{L}_{\text{mask}}$ represents the masked language model loss for sequences. By optimizing these together, the model learns the dependencies between specific amino acid identities and their spatial arrangements.

The architecture is built upon components from AlphaFold 3, including a Pairformer stack and a Joint Diffusion Module that leverages atom-level attention. Instead of a multiple sequence alignment (MSA) module, DISCO incorporates a frozen protein language model (pLM) to provide evolutionary context for partial sequences. Crucially, the model incorporates $SE(3)$ symmetries through data augmentation, ensuring that the generated structures are invariant to rotation and translation in 3D space.

## Cross-Modal Recycling and Inference Optimization

A primary difficulty in co-designing sequence and structure is ensuring they remain consistent throughout the generative trajectory. DISCO introduces "cross-modal recycling" to solve this. At each step $t$ of the diffusion process, the model is conditioned on four pieces of information: the current noisy sequence $s_t$, the current noisy structure $x_t$, and the model’s own predicted "clean" versions of the sequence and structure from the previous step. This feedback loop allows sequence predictions to adapt to the emerging backbone and vice versa.

To further improve the quality of generated proteins, the researchers implemented several inference-time enhancements:

1.  **Sequence Self-Correction:** Standard masked diffusion models often "commit" to a residue once it is unmasked, even if subsequent structural changes make that residue a poor fit. DISCO employs a random remasking strategy during inference, allowing the model to reconsider and correct its sequence choices as the structure matures.
2.  **Entropy-Adaptive Sequence Temperature:** Early in the generative process, models can become overly confident in certain residues. DISCO uses an adaptive temperature scheme that flattens the distribution of tokens when entropy is high, preventing premature commitment and maintaining flexibility during co-design.
3.  **Noisy Guidance:** This is a variation of classifier-free guidance. Instead of using a completely unconditioned model, DISCO uses a model conditioned on a partially noised target. This allows the guidance signal to be more subtle and effective during the early stages of generation.

## Multimodal Steering with Feynman-Kac Correctors

While standard diffusion models excel at sampling from a learned distribution, they do not inherently allow for the optimization of specific properties. To address this, the researchers introduced Multimodal Feynman-Kac Correctors (FKC). These correctors allow the model to steer the generative process toward proteins that satisfy specific rewards or constraints.

FKC-MM (Multimodal) allows for joint optimization of objectives defined over both sequence and structure. For example, if a designer wants to increase the number of disulfide bonds or cation-π interactions, FKC-MM "tilts" the diffusion trajectory to favor those outcomes. Experimental results showed that this method could generate proteins with six disulfide bonds in a 100-residue chain, a density found in only 0.2% of natural proteins.

Another variant, FKC-SG (Specificity Guidance), steers the model toward binding a specific target while simultaneously "repelling" a structurally similar decoy. This is achieved by penalizing the likelihood of the generated design under an off-target model. In tests, this approach successfully distinguished between very similar molecules, such as the amino acids valine and proline, or the steroid hormones aldosterone and cortisone.

## Benchmarking and Structural Realism

In computational evaluations, DISCO demonstrated significant improvements over existing protein design methods. When generating proteins unconditionally, approximately 90% of the sequences were predicted to refold to their designed backbones with an $\text{RMSD} < 2\text{ \AA}$. Compared to models like RFDiffusion or BoltzGen, DISCO achieved higher sequence and structural diversity without sacrificing this "co-designability."

The proteins generated by DISCO exhibit high physicochemical realism. They possess natural amino acid compositions, diverse secondary structures (alpha helices and beta sheets), and native-like surface properties, such as charge distribution and hydrophobicity. 

When conditioned on small molecules, DISCO creates ligand-responsive pockets. The model's binding sites naturally correlate in lipophilicity with the hydrophobicity of the ligand. Furthermore, DISCO is capable of sampling valid ligand conformers, exploring rotatable bonds while maintaining the rigid core geometries of the conditioning molecules. Analysis of the generated binding motifs using structural search tools revealed that the majority of DISCO's active sites are novel; over 80% showed an $\text{RMSD} > 3\text{ \AA}$ when compared to the five nearest residues in known natural proteins.

## Designing New-to-Nature Enzymes for Carbene-Transfer Reactions

The most rigorous test of DISCO's capabilities was the design of functional enzymes for carbene-transfer reactions. These are non-biological reactions involving highly reactive intermediates that are prized in organic synthesis but difficult to control.

The researchers conditioned DISCO on the geometry of an iron-carbenoid intermediate (a heme-cofactor bound to a reactive carbenoid). Notably, they did not provide theozymes or pre-specify which residues should coordinate the reaction. From $10^4$ initial designs, 90 were selected for experimental validation in *Escherichia coli*. The designs were tested against four reactions:
1.  **Alkene cyclopropanation:** One design achieved a 72% yield and a total turnover number ($\text{TTN}$) of 4,050, with a $99:1$ diastereomeric ratio, outperforming previous enzymes developed through rounds of directed evolution.
2.  **B–H insertion:** The top design reached a 98% yield and 5,170 $\text{TTN}$.
3.  **C(sp^3)\text{--}H$ insertion:** One design achieved 2,360 $\text{TTN}$, comparable to performance levels that previously required 14 rounds of directed evolution to achieve in natural variants.
4.  **Spirocyclopropanation:** This challenging reaction yielded multiple active variants with modest turnover.

The successful designs were structurally diverse and often based on protein folds unrelated to catalysis. For example, one of the most active enzymes for spirocyclopropanation was structurally similar to a TetR-family transcription factor that has no known catalytic function in nature. This demonstrates DISCO's ability to repurpose entire protein architectures for completely new chemical tasks.

## Evolvability and the Future of Programmable Proteins

Beyond initial activity, a critical feature of a designed enzyme is its "evolvability"—the ability to be further improved through laboratory evolution. The researchers subjected a DISCO-designed enzyme to a single round of error-prone PCR. This simple evolutionary step led to variants with improved enantioselectivity, with one variant increasing its enantiomeric excess from $35\%$ to $49\%$. The mutations that provided these improvements were scattered throughout the protein, suggesting that the designed enzymes occupy stable, evolvable regions of the sequence-structure landscape.

The significance of this work lies in its ability to bypass the "initial activity" bottleneck that has long hindered the development of biocatalysts for new-to-nature chemistry. By treating protein sequence and structure as a single, co-designed unit and conditioning them on arbitrary chemical targets, DISCO provides a general platform for creating functional biological molecules. This moves the field closer to a "DNA-encoding of chemistry," where any synthetic transformation could potentially be facilitated by a custom-designed, genetically encodable enzyme.

keyInsights: Simultaneously generating protein sequence and structure, conditioned on arbitrary biomolecules without pre-specifying catalytic residues, allows for a broader and more effective exploration of the chemical design space.
Advanced inference strategies are crucial for improving the quality, diversity, and co-designability of generated proteins in multimodal diffusion models, leading to significant gains in performance.

Multimodal inference steering techniques enable controllable search and direct optimization of complex protein properties, transforming protein generation from passive sampling to goal-directed design.
originalProblem: Traditional protein engineering, like directed evolution, struggles to design enzymes for new-to-nature chemistries due to the absence of natural starting points and the labor-intensive nature of identifying initial active proteins.
Current computational enzyme design methods often rely on pre-specified active site geometries or predefined theozymes, limiting the discovery of novel catalytic motifs and mechanisms.

Existing validated generative pipelines decouple protein backbone generation from sequence design (inverse folding), which is suboptimal as protein sequence and 3D structure are fundamentally linked in determining function.
results: DISCO achieved high co-designability (approximately 90% refolding accuracy) and demonstrated superior diversity and novelty compared to existing methods in unconditional and conditional protein generation.

Multiple functional carbene transferases were designed for four new-to-nature reactions, with the top variant achieving 98% yield and 5,170 total turnover number for B-H insertion, outperforming previous engineered and computationally designed catalysts.

Designed enzymes exhibited novel active-site geometries (over 80% with >3 Å RMSD from natural homologs) and were shown to be evolvable, with a single round of error-prone PCR yielding variants with improved activity or inverted enantioselectivity.
solution: Introduced DISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design), a multimodal generative framework that simultaneously models discrete sequences and 3D structures using parallel masked discrete and continuous diffusion processes.

Incorporated key inference strategy enhancements including cross-modal recycling, sequence self-correction via random remasking, and an entropy-adaptive sequence temperature for improved co-designability and error correction.

Developed Multimodal Feynman-Kac Correctors (FKC-MM and FKC-SG) for inference steering, allowing direct optimization of desired properties (e.g., disulfide bonds, binding specificity) during the generative process.
summary.summary: A multimodal generative model named DISCO was developed to simultaneously design protein sequences and 3D structures, enabling the creation of enzymes for new-to-nature chemical reactions without predefined active sites. It successfully generated highly active carbene transferases, achieving up to 98% yield and 5,170 total turnover numbers for transformations like B-H insertion, and demonstrating evolvability for further optimization.

title: General Multimodal Protein Design Enables DNA-Encoding of Chemistry
